title: Yellow Pus in Dog's Ear: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog's ear oozing yellow pus? Learn about ear infections (otitis), when to see a vet, and how triage photos help diagnose the issue.
content_html:
TL;DR: Yellow pus in a dog's ear indicates a bacterial infection that needs veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours. While not usually an immediate life-threatening emergency, prompt treatment is necessary to relieve pain and prevent permanent hearing loss.

What does it mean if my dog has yellow pus in their ear?

Yellow pus, also known as purulent discharge, is a hallmark sign of a bacterial ear infection, clinically referred to as otitis externa. This discharge is composed of white blood cells, cellular debris, and bacteria that have multiplied within the warm, moist environment of the ear canal. It is often accompanied by a distinct, unpleasant odor and significant inflammation.

Is yellow discharge in my dog's ear a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for this condition is Medium; it is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency but is highly uncomfortable and painful for your dog.
  • If left untreated, the infection can migrate to the middle or inner ear, potentially causing permanent hearing loss or balance issues.
  • You should seek veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours to prevent the infection from worsening or rupturing the eardrum.

How can a photo of my dog's ear help with triage?

  • Taking a clear, well-lit photo allows a vet to visualize the volume of discharge and the color of the tissue.
  • A photo helps determine the degree of swelling (stenosis) in the ear canal.
  • This visual evidence helps a professional decide if your pet needs an urgent fit-in appointment or a routine opening.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, yellow pus in the ear canal, or suppurative exudation, often indicates bacterial infection, particularly chronic cases caused by resistant organisms like Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus intermedius or Pseudomonas aeruginosa. These infections are frequently associated with severe epithelial ulceration, pain, and canal edema. The presence of yellow discharge can also be seen with otitis media, where the external ear canal may be inflamed and contain abnormal discharge; head shaking, scratching, head tilting, pain, and odor may be present. Cytology should be performed to identify the causative organism and guide appropriate antimicrobial therapy, especially when neutrophils or rod-type bacteria are present. Systemic antibiotics should be considered in cases of therapeutic failure with topical agents, chronic recurring infections, and all cases of otitis media.

Chapter: Dermatology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 530)

faqs.question: Can I clean my dog's ear at home before the vet visit?
faqs.answer: It is generally best to wait. If the eardrum is ruptured, putting certain cleaners or water into the ear can cause neurological damage. Your vet will perform an otoscopic exam to ensure the eardrum is intact before cleaning.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms of an ear infection to look for?
faqs.answer: Common signs include frequent head shaking, scratching at the ear, rubbing the ear against furniture, redness, and sensitivity when the head is touched.

faqs.question: Can ear infections be treated with over-the-counter medicine?
faqs.answer: No. Most yellow pus discharges require prescription-strength antibiotics or antifungal medications tailored to the specific type of bacteria or yeast present.
